13.reason: The variable num is reset to 0 here, which causes the exponent calculation loop below to overwrite the coefficients stored in ans[0...], and results in num holding only the count of exponents (which equals the count of coefficients) instead of the total number of terms. Furthermore, the coefficients and exponents are stored in separate passes without linking them to the same struct instance correctly if num wasn't reset, but since num IS reset, the coefficients calculated in the first loop are lost/overwritten by the exponents in the second loop because they both start writing at ans[0].

47.reason: [sum() modifies ans in-place but returns an index; however, the logic assumes that after merging, only ans[k] is valid, but sum() merges into ans[k+1], so printing ans[k] prints the zeroed/old term. Also, recursive sum doesn't handle multiple merges properly.]
